title: Python evaluates a default argument once, so a mutable default is shared across calls
rule: A function's default argument is evaluated a single time when the def runs, not per call, so a mutable default (list, dict, set) persists and accumulates state between separate calls. Use None as the sentinel and build the real default inside the body: def f(x, acc=None): acc = [] if acc is None else acc.
triggers: a Python function's default list or dict keeps values from previous calls
def f(x=[]) accumulates items across separate calls
mutable default argument is shared between invocations
Python optional list parameter is not empty on a fresh call
